titleStmt.title: Letter from Peter Mooney to his sister Katie Mooney, 16 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Peter Mooney

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
A letter from Peter Mooney (b.1886) to his sister, Katie (b.1884). Peter thanks Katie for the various items she sent to him, concluding that she is minding him very well. Peter writes the weather is beautiful, he could happily live in such conditions. He believes that he will be granted leave on the 23 of January and warns that when he comes back, he'll eat a 'terrible feed of eggs and rashers.'
Orphaned at age 7, Peter and his sister Katie were raised by their uncle William and his wife Mary in Caulstown. While there are no surviving enlistment papers for Peter, his War Badge Index Record shows his enlistment date as 18 October 1915. He served with the Royal Artillery and later transferred to the Royal Engineers where he was promoted to the rank of Sergeant. He served in several places at the Western Front, including Ypres, St. Omer and Passchendael. Peter and Kate were avid correspondents. This letter part of a collection of over ninety surviving letters.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Jan 16 1916 Dear Katie, I received all the
things you sent me alright
you are certainly minding
me very well. I think
we get leave on the 23rd
of the month. The weather
here is glorious it is
lovely out in the mornings
with all the birds
singing in the trees I
could live here for a lifetime
we were out with the
teams every day this
week. I am doing well
at the business and
enjoy it well. I am
sure Billy & the Cockey
horse would cause
some row if they were
put in the teams.
I am very glad to see
you gave poor Charley
the pound he deserves
it. I will eat a terrible
feed of eggs & rashers
when I get home.
I had not a letter from
the Paddock this long
time. I hope they are all
well. I wrote to them
last Sunday but I got no
reply yet. I will keep
out of old Dunnes way
when I get home he might
hit me with the meat
chopper. Tell Paddy I
was asking for him. The
days are getting grand &
long now. I have no more
to say at present so Good—Bye
& May God Bless you
all. P. Mooney
Miss K. Mooney
Caulstown
Dunboyne
Co. Meath
